This division of responsibility was not necessarily arbitrary or unfair. It flowed partly from biological differences between the two genders, and partly from the social organization of life in ancient communities. And in those communities, people came to accept this division as natural and stable, because it met economic and social needs.

However, times have changed, and these norms have evolved too. For one, science has long since dispelled the idea that gender differences automatically determine individuals’ capabilities and roles in society. Gender does not preordain one’s talents, interests or aptitudes. For another, contemporary societies no longer organize themselves by very rigid divisions between domestic and public spaces. The economies we live in today often reward creativity, analytical thinking, and the ability to communicate effectively - traits that are not intrinsically linked to any gender.

It’s time to embrace these changes. We live in a world where women can contribute to the economy and public life in an ever-growing number of ways. We live in a world in which men can play much greater roles in the lives of their families, as caregivers and as cooks and as homemakers. And in all of this, neither gender need be dominant. Neither women nor men need to be second-class citizens. It’s time to stop asking what the traditional or natural roles of men and women are, and instead to ask what roles women and men are able to play in contemporary society to build a thriving, equitable and inclusive community.
